core.action: Check stock status field (is_ST or is_*ST flag); exclude ST/*ST stocks from buy candidates unless strategy explicitly targets these; for existing positions, apply 5% price limit instead of 10%

consequence.description: ST/*ST stocks have restricted trading windows and 5% daily price limits; ignoring these restrictions leads to impossible backtest trades and potential compliance violations in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-016

core.when: When processing historical price data that includes ex-dividend or ex-right dates
core.action: Assume the framework automatically adjusts prices for dividends and rights issues — unadjusted prices will cause calculation errors in return and technical indicator computations

consequence.description: Without ex-dividend adjustment, historical prices include value that no longer exists; returns are overstated and technical indicators produce false signals around dividend dates

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-017

core.when: When using historical price data for return calculations and technical analysis
core.action: Apply dividend/rights adjustment: for each ex-dividend date, adjust historical prices by multiplying by (close - dividend) / close before that date; use adjusted prices for each return and indicator calculations

consequence.description: Dividend adjustments typically cause 1-5% price drops on ex-dates; unadjusted data makes backtest returns look better than reality and distorts technical indicator calculations

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-017

core.when: When selecting stocks for trading in early listing periods
core.action: Assume the framework handles new stock trading restrictions — new stocks (first 44 trading days in China) have special trading rules including price limits and trading halts

consequence.description: New stocks have special trading rules for the first 44 trading days; without handling, backtest assumes trades are executable when they may be halted or subject to price limits

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-018

core.when: When backtesting index-linked strategies that require accurate index composition
core.action: Assume the framework automatically updates index constituent changes — stale index composition data causes wrong stock selections and significant tracking errors

consequence.description: Index rebalancing dates are missed if the framework does not track constituent changes; strategies tracking indexes will have wrong positions and poor tracking performance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-019

core.when: When implementing index-linked or index-enhanced strategies
core.action: Synchronize index constituent data: download index change announcements, apply constituent additions/removals effective on rebalancing dates, rebalance portfolio within 1 trading day of index change

consequence.description: Index changes are typically known in advance; failing to anticipate constituent changes causes tracking error and missed alpha opportunities around rebalancing dates

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-019

core.when: When computing covariance matrices for portfolio optimization
core.action: Assume the covariance matrix is always positive semi-definite (PSD) — sample covariance matrices are often non-PSD due to estimation error, causing optimization failures

consequence.description: Non-PSD covariance matrices cause portfolio optimizers to fail or produce unrealistic portfolios with extreme long/short positions; the optimization is mathematically undefined

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-020

core.when: When building covariance matrices for mean-variance portfolio optimization
core.action: Apply PSD repair after covariance estimation: use nearest correlation matrix method (Higham 2002) or eigenvalue clipping to verify each eigenvalues >= 0; verify matrix is PSD before passing to optimizer

consequence.description: Covariance matrices with negative eigenvalues cause numerical instability in optimizers; Higham's nearest correlation method ensures valid matrices while preserving correlation structure

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-020

core.when: When selecting covariance estimation methods for portfolio construction
core.action: Assume the sample covariance is optimal — sample covariance is unstable with high-dimensional data, leading to noisy optimal portfolios that underperform out-of-sample

consequence.description: Sample covariance with N assets and limited history (N > T) is singular or near-singular; portfolios built on this matrix have extreme weights that fail in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-021

core.when: When estimating covariance matrices for portfolios with many assets
core.action: Use shrinkage estimators when N/T ratio > 0.5: apply Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage with optimal shrinkage intensity, or use factor-based covariance (e.g., 3-factor model) to stabilize estimates

consequence.description: Shrinkage estimators reduce estimation error by pulling sample covariance toward structured targets; optimal shrinkage intensity typically reduces out-of-sample portfolio variance by 5-15%

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-021

core.when: When calculating VaR/CVaR for risk reporting and limit monitoring
core.action: Explicitly set and justify confidence_level and window parameters: for regulatory VaR use 99% confidence with 1-day or 10-day window; for internal risk management use 95% confidence with longer lookback; document rationale for parameter selection

consequence.description: VaR parameters must align with risk mandate: 99%/1-day for Basel compliance, 95%/historical window for internal risk; mismatched parameters lead to wrong risk estimates and inadequate capital buffers

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-022

core.when: When implementing volatility modeling in the framework
core.action: Assume the framework implements volatility model family selection and distribution assumptions — the framework does not include volatility model implementations

consequence.description: Without explicit volatility model selection, the framework may default to naive assumptions causing systematic underestimation of tail risk in stress scenarios

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-023

core.when: When calculating factor IC and forming portfolio groups
core.action: Demean factor IC values within each formation period and verify temporal alignment between factor formation and portfolio grouping windows using matching date ranges

consequence.description: Without demean and alignment, IC calculations contain look-ahead bias causing strategy backtests to show inflated returns that cannot be reproduced in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-024

core.action: Assume the framework implements IFRS 9 default definition — the framework does not include IFRS 9-compliant default event definitions or flags

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-025

core.when: When calculating expected credit loss or risk metrics
core.action: Define default events per IFRS 9 Clause 2.1 combining both quantitative (90 days past due) and qualitative triggers (bankruptcy, debt restructuring), implement corresponding default flags, and select estimation methods for PD (cohort/through-the-cycle), LGD (cure rate/inverse of recovery), and EAD (drawdown/collateral adjustment)

consequence.description: Missing PD/LGD/EAD estimation causes expected credit loss to be uncomputable or severely underestimated, violating IFRS 9 impairment requirements

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-026

core.action: Assume the framework implements Vasicek single-factor correlation — the framework does not include correlation parameter implementation for correlated default modeling

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-027

core.when: When applying correlation parameter in credit loss calculations
core.action: Explicitly specify correlation parameter ρ calibrated to asset return correlation or regulatory templates (Basel IRB floor of 0.12) and apply it in credit loss distribution calculations using the Vasicek asymptotic single-factor model formula

consequence.description: Omitting correlation parameter causes diversification benefit overstatement in correlated default scenarios, resulting in understated portfolio loss at tail quantiles

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-027

core.when: When estimating transition matrices for credit rating migrations
core.action: Assume transition matrices remain time-homogeneous by default — the framework does not implement time-varying migration matrix estimation or validation

consequence.description: Assuming time-homogeneous matrices when rating migration patterns shift causes systematic understatement of credit risk during volatile market periods

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-028

core.when: When estimating credit rating transition matrices
core.action: Test for time-varying transition matrices using chi-square homogeneity test or Greene (2007) method; if homogeneity is rejected, adopt time-inhomogeneous matrices or rolling estimation windows

consequence.description: Using time-homogeneous matrices when migration patterns evolve causes inaccurate credit VaR calculations, leading to mispriced risk and insufficient provisions

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-028

core.action: Assume the framework implements stress testing scenarios — the framework does not include built-in stress testing scenario definitions or shock application mechanisms

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-029

core.action: Define at minimum three stress scenarios (baseline, adverse, severely adverse) per regulatory requirements and apply corresponding factor shocks to positions or portfolio using scenario-specific return distributions

consequence.description: Missing stress testing causes backtest results to appear viable while strategy faces catastrophic losses in adverse market conditions like 2008 or March 2020 volatility spikes

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-029

core.action: Populate each 29 EBA NPL template fields per EBA/OR/2020/04 Annex requirements including the 10 mandatory NPL portfolio fields: product type, default date, default status, default amount, collateral type, collateral value, stage classification, provisioning rate, write-off amount, and recovery amount

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-030

core.action: Assume the framework implements data versioning and snapshot semantics — the framework does not include versioned writes or point-in-time data retrieval mechanisms

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-031

core.action: Implement versioning logic using _timestamp columns or manual version tracking to verify each read operation retrieves data as-of the backtest simulation date, not the latest available values

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-031

core.when: When applying data transformations in production ML pipelines
core.action: Assume the framework enforces fit/transform isolation in pipelines — the framework does not implement automatic fit/transform separation or validation

consequence.description: Without fit/transform isolation, fitted transformers may be accidentally refit on live data causing data leakage and invalidating backtest statistical significance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-032

core.when: When implementing ML feature engineering pipelines
core.action: Explicitly separate pipeline stages: fit transformers only on training data within cross-validation splits, apply (transform only) on validation/test/ live data, and use Pipeline.fit() and Pipeline.transform() methods distinctly with validated stage boundaries

consequence.description: Fitting transformers on full dataset causes data leakage where validation performance is artificially high due to information contamination from future data

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-032

core.action: Assume the framework handles covariates time availability constraints — the framework does not implement this validation; missing covariate data may cause silent alignment failures

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-033

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-033

core.action: Implement consensus mechanism: define conflict resolution rules (e.g., majority voting, priority hierarchy, or arbitration layer) before order execution; for conflicting signals on the same asset, execute only the highest-priority agent's decision or aggregate via weighted consensus score

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-034

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-040

core.when: When configuring turbulence threshold parameters for training and deployment environments
core.action: Verify that turbulence thresholds are consistent between training (90th percentile) and deployment (99th percentile); if using different thresholds, re-train agent with deployment threshold or accept behavioral discontinuity during deployment

consequence.description: Training-deployment threshold mismatch causes the agent to encounter a 'no-buy' state during deployment that was never experienced during training; agent behavior exhibits discontinuity when turbulence is between 90th-99th percentile, leading to unexpected trading gaps in live deployment

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-I003

core.when: When using Sharpe ratio (annualized) as the primary model selection criterion
core.action: Use Sharpe ratio as the sole selection criterion when returns exhibit autocorrelation, heavy tails, or non-normal distributions — these conditions violate the i.i.d. normal assumption underlying Sharpe ratio

consequence.description: Sharpe ratio under i.i.d. normal assumption misses tail risk and autocorrelation effects; strategies with high Sharpe but heavy-tailed returns may experience larger drawdowns than Sharpe ratio predicts, causing live trading returns to fall below model selection expectations

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-062

core.action: Use the same turbulence threshold percentile (90th) for both training and trading environment; do not use different percentile values (e.g., 99th) that create regime mismatch

consequence.description: Training with 90th percentile threshold but trading with 99th creates regime mismatch where risk-on/risk-off signals are triggered at different volatility levels, causing the deployed strategy to behave differently than trained

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-008

core.when: When configuring turbulence threshold parameters for regime detection

consequence.description: Silent mismatch between training (90th percentile) and deployment (99th percentile) thresholds causes the strategy to switch regimes at wrong volatility levels, leading to incorrect risk management decisions in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-008

core.when: When computing Sharpe Ratio for strategy performance evaluation
core.action: Use sqrt(252) annualization factor for daily returns only when returns are i.i.d. normal; for non-normal returns (skew, heavy tails) or changing time horizons, use non-annualized Sharpe or alternative metrics (Sortino, Calmar)

consequence.description: Annualizing Sharpe with sqrt(252) on non-i.i.d. returns (e.g., autocorrelated, heteroskedastic) systematically mismeasures risk-adjusted performance, overstating or understating true strategy quality

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-082

core.action: Process SELL orders before BUY orders — verify sell_index is sorted and processed first to release capital before executing buys

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-017

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-033
